institution: Virginia Polytechnic Institute and State University

ai_strategy_lead.name: Dale Pike; David Raymond
ai_strategy_lead.title: Co-chairs, Virginia Tech AI Working Group (Associate Vice Provost for Technology-Enhanced Learning; Associate Vice President for Security and Identity, Division of IT)

documents.title: Responsible and Ethical AI Framework for Virginia Tech (v1.0)

ai_strategy.summary: Virginia Tech publicly released its Responsible and Ethical AI Framework in September 2025, authored by the AI Working Group co-chaired by Dale Pike and David Raymond. The framework codifies seven guiding principles (mission alignment, innovation for good, human-centered benefit, responsible/ethical use, fairness and transparency, human judgment and accountability, data security and privacy). A follow-on October 2025 Strategic AI Investment Proposal targets a Universal Conversational AI platform contract with production implementation by April 1, 2026, plus AI literacy, upskilling, and governance capacity.

key_provisions: Only explicitly approved AI tools may be used with moderate- or high-risk university data
Unapproved AI tools may be used only with low-risk, public data
Three syllabus-level policy templates (permitted with disclosure, prohibited, assignment-specific)
Faculty cautioned against using AI detection software; detection reports not accepted as Honor Code evidence
Users must disclose AI use to affected parties (transparency principle)
Human must remain in the loop for important decisions
Research guidance restricts sharing non-public data with generative AI without a vendor agreement
ai_policy.summary: Virginia Tech's AI policy regime is comprehensive, combining the September 2025 Responsible and Ethical AI Framework, the approved-tools list, TLOS teaching guidance, and research-specific data-risk guidance. The stance is permissive-with-guardrails at the classroom level (faculty set syllabus policy from three templates) but restrictive on data: moderate- or high-risk institutional data can only go into explicitly approved, enterprise-protected tools.

bodies.name: Virginia Tech AI Working Committee
bodies.scope: University-wide standing committee within IT governance; delegated responsibility for AI-related issues; reviews high-risk implementations, develops standards, and recommends tools and processes

bodies.name: AI for Teaching & Learning Subgroup
bodies.scope: Faculty guidance, syllabus templates, and student AI literacy curriculum

bodies.name: AI for Research Subgroup
bodies.scope: Research protocols, intellectual property guidelines, and ethical review for AI in research

bodies.name: AI for Operations Subgroup
bodies.scope: Administrative AI implementations and vendor evaluation

bodies.name: Virginia Tech AI Working Group (original drafting body)
bodies.scope: Authored the 2025 Responsible and Ethical AI Framework; co-chaired by Dale Pike (TLOS) and David Raymond (DoIT Security and Identity); sunset into the standing Working Committee

governance_structure.summary: Virginia Tech's AI governance is organized around a standing AI Working Committee inside the IT governance framework, reporting to the IT Governance Advisory Committee, with three domain subgroups (Teaching & Learning, Research, Operations). The committee emerged from the 2024-2025 AI Working Group that authored the Responsible and Ethical AI Framework. Operational decisions remain with campus leaders; the committee provides advisory stewardship and recommendations.

initiatives.title: Responsible and Ethical AI Framework for Virginia Tech (v1.0)
initiatives.description: The AI Working Group, co-chaired by Dale Pike and David Raymond, published the v1.0 framework in September 2025, codifying seven AI principles and proposing governance structures that became the standing AI Working Committee with Teaching & Learning, Research, and Operations subgroups.
initiatives.significance: One of the more structured public AI frameworks among R1 land-grants, combining principles, governance, and subsequent investment planning in a single coherent package rather than a standalone statement.

investment.description: Working-group labor plus governance capacity to stand up the standing committee and subgroups; specific dollar figure not disclosed in the framework itself.

collaborations.description: Internally drafted across colleges, administrative units, students, researchers, and IT/security staff.

initiatives.policy_implications: Sets the institutional reference point for all subsequent AI tool approvals, research guidance, and teaching policy templates.

initiatives.title: Strategic AI Investment Proposal and Universal Conversational AI contract
initiatives.description: In October 2025 Virginia Tech released a revised Strategic AI Investment Proposal planning a Universal Conversational AI platform contract with production implementation targeted for April 1, 2026, plus funding for AI literacy, upskilling, policy updates, and expanded approved-tool access.
initiatives.significance: Signals that VT is moving beyond pilots to a single enterprise conversational AI layer with formal change-management rather than a scatter of departmental licenses.

investment.description: Multi-year enablement package; exact dollar amount of the conversational AI contract not publicly disclosed.

collaborations.description: Contract award pending; vendor selection to be announced ahead of April 2026 production implementation.

initiatives.policy_implications: Implementation will require policy/procedure updates, new training programs, and expanded access management for a campus-wide conversational AI layer.

initiatives.title: ARC LLM Gateway: university-hosted generative AI for high-risk data
initiatives.description: Advanced Research Computing launched a no-cost LLM Gateway at llm.arc.vt.edu plus Open OnDemand access to LLMs, approved for high-risk data and open to all students, faculty, and staff.
initiatives.significance: Rare among R1s — an institutionally hosted LLM service that can handle high-risk data, reducing dependency on third-party consumer APIs for sensitive research and administrative workloads.

investment.description: Operational cost absorbed by ARC; free to all VT users; built on existing ARC HPC/GPU infrastructure.

tools_and_vendors.name: ARC LLM Gateway
tools_and_vendors.role: university-hosted LLM gateway approved for high-risk data

initiatives.policy_implications: Creates a sanctioned path for high-risk data use with generative AI, reducing shadow-IT use of consumer tools.

initiatives.title: Multi-vendor enterprise AI stack: Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini/NotebookLM, ChatGPT Edu pilot
initiatives.description: VT maintains an unusual multi-vendor stack: Microsoft Copilot Chat (free for all users under Enterprise Data Protection), Microsoft 365 Copilot (department-purchased), Google Gemini and NotebookLM (all users via VT Google accounts), Google AI Pro (department-purchased), and a capped ChatGPT Edu pilot for 500 users.
initiatives.significance: Most R1s standardize on one vendor; VT's concurrent Microsoft + Google + OpenAI approach offers broad faculty choice but expands the governance and training burden materially.

investment.funding_source: Virginia Tech institutional plus departmental licensing
investment.description: Baseline Copilot Chat and Gemini included in enterprise Microsoft/Google contracts; M365 Copilot and Google AI Pro require department purchase; ChatGPT Edu pilot licensed to 500 seats.

collaborations.description: Enterprise licensing and pilot relationships with three major AI vendors simultaneously.

tools_and_vendors.name: Microsoft Copilot Chat
tools_and_vendors.role: free generative AI chat for all VT users under Enterprise Data Protection

tools_and_vendors.name: Google Gemini App
tools_and_vendors.role: generative AI for all VT users via VT Google account

tools_and_vendors.name: Google NotebookLM
tools_and_vendors.role: research/reading assistant for all VT users

initiatives.policy_implications: Broad choice architecture pushes governance and literacy programs to define where each tool is appropriate rather than limiting via procurement.

initiatives.title: AI essay scoring in undergraduate admissions
initiatives.description: For the 2025-26 cycle, Virginia Tech became one of the first major public universities to publicly deploy AI essay scoring for all undergraduate applicants. Each essay is scored by one human reader and one internally trained AI model; if they disagree by more than two points on a 12-point scale, a second human reviews.
initiatives.significance: One of the highest-profile operational uses of AI in admissions by a U.S. R1 — reported to save 8,000+ human reader hours and compress decision timelines by up to a month. Sets a reference precedent for peer institutions weighing similar deployments.

investment.description: Internally developed model trained on historical applicant essays and rubric; cost not publicly disclosed.

collaborations.description: Built internally by Virginia Tech admissions and data teams.

tools_and_vendors.name: Internal essay-scoring model
tools_and_vendors.role: second-reader AI scoring alongside human reviewers

initiatives.policy_implications: Model is advisory — final admission decisions remain with human professionals — but sets governance, bias, and FERPA precedents that other institutions will scrutinize.

initiatives.title: Falcon GPU supercomputer for applied AI research
initiatives.description: Advanced Research Computing brought Falcon online in December 2024, a 52-node GPU cluster with 208 NVIDIA GPUs (128 A30 + 80 L40S with 48 GB per GPU for LLM work), connected via next-generation InfiniBand at 200 Gbps per node.
initiatives.significance: Dedicated GPU capacity tuned for LLMs and scientific AI is increasingly a prerequisite for R1 competitiveness in federal AI grants and industry partnerships.

investment.description: Capital investment in 52 GPU nodes (208 GPUs) plus InfiniBand fabric; specific dollar amount not publicly disclosed.

collaborations.description: NVIDIA A30 and L40S GPUs; standard vendor relationship.

tools_and_vendors.name: Falcon GPU cluster
tools_and_vendors.role: shared GPU supercomputer for applied AI and LLM research

initiatives.title: Institute for Advanced Computing launch at Alexandria Innovation Campus
initiatives.description: In May 2025 VT announced the Institute for Advanced Computing (IAC), anchored at the 11-story, 300,000 sq. ft. Academic Building One in Alexandria. The institute houses the Sanghani Center for AI and Data Analytics, AI/ML, next-G wireless, quantum, and intelligent-interface research in close proximity to Amazon HQ2 and the federal government.
initiatives.significance: Couples AI capacity to the National Landing innovation cluster and federal market — among the largest single R1 building investments in AI/computing in the last five years and tied directly to Virginia's $1B Tech Talent Investment after Amazon HQ2.

investment.funding_source: Commonwealth of Virginia Tech Talent Investment Program
investment.description: Part of Virginia's $1B higher-education investment catalyzed by Amazon HQ2; building opened spring 2025; institute formally announced May 2025.

collaborations.description: Located in National Landing adjacent to Amazon HQ2; anchor tenant for VT's federal-facing research presence.

initiatives.title: Amazon-Virginia Tech Initiative for Efficient and Robust Machine Learning
initiatives.description: Launched in 2022 and still active, the initiative funds ML research, Amazon Fellows doctoral fellowships (2nd-4th-year engineering PhDs with Amazon internships), and faculty-led projects. Directed by Naren Ramakrishnan, with 2023-24 and subsequent annual awards of fellows and 4-5 faculty projects per year.
initiatives.significance: One of the most durable named industry-academic AI partnerships in U.S. higher education, persisting through the HQ2 construction period and across multiple leadership changes.

investment.description: Multi-year Amazon funding through the College of Engineering and Sanghani Center; annual fellow stipends plus project grants. Specific annual dollar amount not publicly disclosed.

collaborations.description: Research projects, fellowships, shared advisory board, community outreach.

initiatives.title: Commonwealth Cyber Initiative (CCI) AI cybersecurity portfolio
initiatives.description: Virginia Tech leads the CCI Southwest Node and shares in CCI's 2025 round of 18 AI-cybersecurity seed grants ($1.61M across nine Virginia public universities). VT is a co-PI on projects on anomaly detection for network traffic, zero-trust explainable AI, and synthetic iris biometrics.
initiatives.significance: Positions VT as the Virginia hub for AI-security research and demonstrates a sustainable cross-institution model that could be replicated by other states.

investment.description: $1.61M across 18 seed grants in 2025; VT is a co-PI on several and leads the Southwest Node.

collaborations.description: Multi-institution CCI collaborative projects funded by Commonwealth of Virginia.

initiatives.title: M.S. in Data Science launched at Innovation Campus (Fall 2025)
initiatives.description: Virginia Tech launched a new Master of Science in Data Science in Fall 2025 as an accelerated 4+1 program, with full-time Blacksburg and part-time Northern Virginia options launching Fall 2026.
initiatives.significance: Expands VT's data/AI workforce pipeline beyond the existing M.A. in Data Analysis and graduate certificates, with an emphasis on applied projects and industry capstones.

investment.funding_source: Virginia Tech College of Science and College of Engineering
investment.description: New master's degree; tuition-funded; staffing and capstone partnerships ongoing.

collaborations.description: Second-year capstone pairs students with industry projects.

initiatives.title: AI Undergraduate Curriculum Development Grants (2025-26)
initiatives.description: The Office of Undergraduate Curriculum, CETL, and TLOS jointly funded grants (up to $10,000 per proposal, $5,000 per faculty) to develop or substantially revise undergraduate courses, majors, or minors to be AI-centric. Submission deadline December 5, 2025. Recipients announced include Chris Frank and Yulia R. Gel in the Department of Statistics.
initiatives.significance: Rather than a single AI-major launch, VT is funding distributed AI curriculum development across disciplines — a sector-relevant alternative pattern to the standalone AI-degree approach.

investment.funding_source: Virginia Tech Office of the Provost / CETL / TLOS
investment.description: Competitive faculty grants up to $10,000 per proposal; aggregate budget not publicly disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: Incentivizes department-level curriculum change rather than top-down new-degree launches.

initiatives.title: Faculty AI literacy and professional development via TLOS/CETL
initiatives.description: Virginia Tech offers a structured AI faculty development stack through TLOS and CETL: 'Introduction to Generative AI' (1 credit, self-paced), 'Foundational AI Literacy for Educators' (1 credit, self-paced), plus in-class AI and digital ethics workshops on request and ongoing consultations.
initiatives.significance: Systematic faculty literacy infrastructure, rather than ad-hoc training, positions VT ahead of peers on instructor readiness — a precondition for durable classroom adoption.

investment.description: Staff time plus platform costs; courses are free to VT faculty.

initiatives.policy_implications: Supports the syllabus-policy templates by giving faculty the literacy needed to make informed decisions.

initiatives.title: AI in Research Guidance (VPR/SIRC)
initiatives.description: The Office of the Vice President for Research and the Scholarly Integrity and Research Compliance (SIRC) office published 'Considerations for the Responsible and Ethical Use of AI' and 'Guidance: Using Artificial Intelligence During Research Activities' — restricting use of non-public research data in consumer AI tools and flagging IRB, IP, and sponsor-agreement implications.
initiatives.significance: Brings AI compliance in line with existing research-integrity regimes; rare level of specificity on data classification vs. AI tool choice.

investment.funding_source: Virginia Tech Office of Research and Innovation
investment.description: Guidance documents and compliance support; no dedicated dollar figure disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: Anchors AI use in the existing research integrity framework rather than creating a parallel track.

initiatives.title: Machine Learning undergraduate major (Computer Engineering)
initiatives.description: Bradley Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering offers a Machine Learning major as a formal undergraduate degree pathway inside Computer Engineering.
initiatives.significance: Provides a clear undergraduate ML pipeline feeding into VT's graduate data science and AI programs, without requiring a standalone 'AI major'.

investment.description: Established undergraduate program in Bradley ECE.

computing_infrastructure.supercomputer_name: TinkerCliffs (flagship CPU) and Falcon (GPU for AI/LLM); Owl (water-cooled CPU); CUI (A100 restricted)
computing_infrastructure.summary: Advanced Research Computing (ARC) operates a multi-cluster HPC environment. Falcon (2024) is the applied-AI GPU cluster with 208 GPUs (128 NVIDIA A30 + 80 NVIDIA L40S) on 200 Gbps InfiniBand. TinkerCliffs is the CPU flagship (≈42,000 cores, 93+ TB RAM), with 112 NVIDIA A100 and 56 NVIDIA H200 GPUs reported for expanded AI work. CUI (24 A100) serves ITAR/CUI workloads and Biomed serves NIST 800-171 biomedical workloads. Owl (2024) adds water-cooled CPU capacity. ARC also hosts the no-cost LLM Gateway at llm.arc.vt.edu.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: No-cost AI tools for all VT users
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Free Microsoft Copilot Chat, Google Gemini, NotebookLM, and ARC LLM Gateway access for every VT student, faculty, and staff member — explicitly reduces pay-to-access disparities.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Faculty AI detection guidance (anti-over-enforcement)
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: The Office for Undergraduate Academic Integrity does not accept AI detection reports as Honor Code evidence and advises faculty against using AI detection software — mitigates documented bias of AI detectors against non-native English writers.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: AI accessibility architecture (April 2026)
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: New project to study accessibility-architecture implications of AI tools for disabled users, announced April 2026.

regulatory_context.summary: Governor Glenn Youngkin issued Executive Order 30 on January 18, 2024, establishing state AI guidelines, a $600K pilot budget, and an AI Task Force. The order's standards explicitly exclude research and development at public higher education institutions, but VITA's June 2024 standards and subsequent guidance shape how public universities interact with state agencies and citizens. In March 2025, the governor vetoed HB 2094 (algorithmic discrimination bill), leaving Virginia without a comprehensive private-sector AI law. VT is not part of a university-system governance structure, so there is no 'system AI policy' layer.

controversies_and_litigation.summary: National press coverage raised bias, fairness, and disclosure questions about VT's deployment of AI essay scoring in undergraduate admissions — one of the first R1 public admissions AI deployments to be widely scrutinized.

narrative_summary: Virginia Polytechnic Institute and State University (Virginia Tech) sits among the most structured R1 AI postures in the country as of April 2026. In September 2025 the university published a Responsible and Ethical AI Framework authored by an AI Working Group co-chaired by Dale Pike (TLOS) and David Raymond (DoIT Security and Identity); the framework codifies seven AI principles and a standing AI Working Committee with three subgroups (Teaching & Learning, Research, Operations). A follow-on October 2025 Strategic AI Investment Proposal set an April 2026 target for a Universal Conversational AI platform contract with full change-management plans. Enterprise tooling is unusually broad — Microsoft Copilot Chat and Google Gemini/NotebookLM are free for all users under enterprise data protection, M365 Copilot and Google AI Pro are licensed at the department level, a 500-seat ChatGPT Edu pilot is running, and Advanced Research Computing hosts its own ARC LLM Gateway that is approved for high-risk data. Compute capacity is meaningful: ARC's Falcon GPU cluster came online in late 2024 with 208 NVIDIA GPUs on 200 Gbps InfiniBand, complementing TinkerCliffs, Owl, CUI, and Biomed. Research strength clusters around the Sanghani Center (2011) now headquartered in the new Alexandria Institute for Advanced Computing (launched May 2025 on the back of Virginia's $1B Amazon HQ2 Tech Talent Investment), the multi-year Amazon-Virginia Tech ML Initiative directed by Naren Ramakrishnan, and the Commonwealth Cyber Initiative's 2025 AI cybersecurity portfolio ($1.61M across nine Virginia publics). Workforce pipeline includes a new M.S. in Data Science (Fall 2025), an ML undergraduate major, data analytics certificates, a bootcamp, and AI Undergraduate Curriculum Development Grants up to $10K per proposal. Student experience is marked by the high-profile Fall 2025 deployment of AI essay scoring for all undergraduate applicants under VP Juan Espinoza — probably the most-cited AI-in-admissions R1 case and an active public discussion about fairness and disclosure. The state context (Youngkin's 2024 EO 30 and 2025 veto of HB 2094) leaves VT with room to set its own pace; the Virginia higher-education carve-out in EO 30 means state AI rules don't override university research. Gaps: no standalone Chief AI Officer; no public full budget for the Strategic AI Investment Proposal; limited public documentation of student-services chatbot deployments; and the conversational-AI vendor selection remained undisclosed as of the research date.
